planting.note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant Red Runner tubers 4-6 inches deep once soil warms, laying each tuber horizontally with the eye pointing up. This decorative variety produces dark romantic-red blooms on strong stems well suited for cutting. Pinch the growing tip at 12 inches to encourage bushier plants and more flowers. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter. Water deeply once sprouts emerge and avoid overwatering newly planted tubers.

planting.note: Set tubers out after the last spring frost when the soil is warm.

directions: Rip City bears dramatic dark red-black blooms on long, easy-to-cut stems from early in the season. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after frost danger passes, and stake the plants early since the tall stems benefit from support. This variety is a strong tuber producer, so plants can be divided at season's end. Deadhead spent flowers to keep new blooms coming through fall. Lift tubers before hard frost in cold-winter regions.

planting.note: Plant after frost when soil reaches about 60 F.

directions: Robin Hood produces large salmon-orange blooms whose saturation shifts through the growing season, making each flower slightly different. Because the big blooms are heavy, stake or corral this variety early so stems stay upright. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger has passed. Feed with a low-nitrogen fertilizer to favor flowers over foliage. Cut blooms freely to encourage continued production.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Rock Run Ashley is a smaller smoky-salmon dahlia that blends beautifully in mixed bouquets and is a favorite with florists. Plant tubers horizontally 4-6 inches deep after the soil warms, spacing plants about 12-18 inches apart. Pinch young plants to promote branching and more cut stems. Keep soil evenly moist during bloom but let it dry slightly between waterings to prevent tuber rot. Harvest flowers in the cool of the morning for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Plant tubers outdoors once the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Rockstar is a dark anemone-type dahlia with distinctive foliage that blooms continuously through the season and holds well in the vase. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil after frost, positioning the eye upward. Provide full sun and support tall stems as needed. Deadhead regularly and cut blooms often to keep the plant productive. In cold climates dig and store tubers before the first hard freeze.

planting.note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for a midsummer harvest.

directions: Romanian Red is a porcelain hardneck garlic that yields large, dense bulbs of usually 4-5 easy-to-peel cloves with a hot, robust flavor and excellent storage. Separate bulbs into cloves just before planting and set each clove pointed end up, 2 inches deep and 4-6 inches apart, in fall. Mulch heavily after the ground cools to protect against heaving over winter. Snap off the curling scapes in early summer to direct energy into bulb sizing. Harvest when the lower leaves brown, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy spot for two to three weeks.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring once the soil is workable.

directions: Romeo and Juliet are a pair of compact, extremely cold-hardy bush cherries that produce sweet, robust fruit good for snacking and baking. Plant both varieties near each other in full sun with well-drained soil to improve cross-pollination and yields. Space plants 5-8 feet apart and water consistently while young to establish strong roots. Prune lightly in late winter to maintain an open, productive shape. These dwarf trees stay small enough for backyard gardens and fruit reliably even in harsh winter regions.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Salmon Runner is a well-balanced salmon-colored dahlia that produces abundant cut flowers without needing staking. Plant the tuber 4-6 inches deep in loose, fertile soil after the last frost, eye facing up. This sturdy variety stands on its own but still benefits from pinching to increase stem count. Water deeply and consistently once growth is established, and cut blooms often to prolong flowering. Lift and store tubers over winter in regions colder than zone 8.

planting.note: Direct sow after all danger of frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Scarlet Emperor is a vigorous pole runner bean prized for its brilliant clusters of edible red flowers as well as its rich-flavored pods. Sow seeds 1 inch deep and 4-6 inches apart at the base of a tall trellis, fence, or pole after the soil warms in spring. Pick young pods for tender snap beans, or leave them to mature and shell for green or dried beans. Keep vines watered during flowering and harvest regularly to keep new pods forming. This variety doubles as an ornamental screen thanks to its showy blooms that attract hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Snoho Doris is a tall, productive dahlia bearing salmon-coral blooms with uniquely patterned petals on very long, easy-to-harvest stems. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained soil after the last frost, and provide light support for the tall plants. Pinch the central shoot early to encourage more branching and stems. Cut flowers frequently to encourage continued blooming into fall. A great choice for beginning dahlia growers thanks to its reliable production.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last spring frost when soil warms.

directions: Snoho Jojo is a productive, short-statured dahlia with smoky mauve blooms carried on long stems that are excellent for cutting. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil once frost danger passes. The compact plant produces cut-worthy stems from the start of the season. Deadhead and cut regularly to keep blooms coming through fall. In cold climates, lift the tubers before the first hard freeze and store them in a cool, dry place.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring once the soil can be worked.

directions: Tasty Red is a narrow, columnar apple that produces sweet, bite-sized red fruit with a refreshing crunch while taking up very little space. Its upright growth habit makes it ideal for containers, patios, and small yards, and several can be planted close together. Set the tree in full sun with well-drained soil and water regularly during establishment. Plant a second apple variety nearby for cross-pollination and better yields. Minimal pruning is needed thanks to the naturally compact columnar form.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes in the garden, or grow in a container to move indoors in cold winters.

directions: Camellia sinensis is the evergreen shrub whose young leaves are processed into green, black, and oolong tea. Grow it in acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun, sheltered from harsh wind. Water regularly to keep the soil evenly moist but never waterlogged, and mulch to protect the shallow roots. In cold-winter areas, grow the plant in a container so it can be moved to protection. Harvest the tender top two leaves and bud from established plants, then dry or process them to make your own tea.

planting.note: Plant tubers outdoors after the last frost when the soil is warm.

directions: Terracotta produces romantic terracotta-bronze blooms with wavy petals on long stems, making it an outstanding cut flower and a strong tuber producer. Plant tubers 4-6 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil once frost has passed, laying them horizontally with the eye up. Provide full sun and stake tall plants to keep the long cutting stems upright. Cut flowers frequently and deadhead to encourage continuous bloom into fall. Dig and store the tubers before hard frost in cold climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger passes, or grow in a patio container in colder zones.

directions: Texas Pink is a pomegranate valued for its large rosy-pink blushed fruit and its ornamental, gnarled bonsai-like bark and bright orange-red flowers. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, allowing plenty of room for the shrubby tree to develop. Once established, it is quite drought-tolerant and needs only occasional deep watering. In zones colder than 7, grow it in a patio container and move it to shelter over winter. Prune to a single trunk or multi-stemmed form and thin fruit to improve size.
